text: Upon entering the abdominal cavity, the surgeon encounters severe inflammatory adhesions between the colon and the gallbladder and proceeds with adhesiolysis to separate these structures.
The surgeon opens the colon during adhesiolysis due to the presence of a cholecystocolonic fistula, revealing the fistula connection between the gallbladder and colon.
The surgeon uses monopolar scissors to carefully mobilize the colon from the gallbladder, separating the two structures with precise dissection.
The gallbladder appears as a fibrotic and sclerotic mass, indicating severe chronic inflammation and tissue changes.
The surgeon successfully opens a plane between the colon and the gallbladder, moving beyond the area of severe inflammation and adhesions.
The surgeon further mobilizes the colon from the liver and the hepatoduodenal ligament, preparing to close the fistula between the colon and the gallbladder.
The surgeon closes the opening in the colon, which was created during the adhesiolysis of the cholecystocolonic fistula, before proceeding with the cholecystectomy.
The surgeon has completely mobilized the colon, and is now dissecting the borders of the opening between the colon and the gallbladder, preparing for closure.
The surgeon manually closes a large opening in the transverse colon to prevent stenosis, ensuring the colon's lumen remains unobstructed.
The surgeon prepares to perform a Heineken Mikulić Structuroplasty technique on the colon, focusing on the area previously mobilized and dissected.
The surgeon places two corner stitches using V-Lock II-O suture material to begin closing the opening in the colon.
The surgeon creates the inner layer of the colon closure using a V-Lock III-O suture in a continuous running fashion, ensuring the edges of the opening are securely approximated.
The surgeon uses barbed sutures to perform a running suture on the dissected borders of the colon opening, allowing for continuous traction during closure.
The surgeon completes the inner layer and proceeds to reinforce the closure with a second outer layer using previously placed stitches, ensuring the colon transversum is securely closed.
The surgeon ties the two stitches of the outer layer, completing the closure of the colon with visible sutures securing the tissue.
The surgeon performs a small wedge resection of liver segments IVb and Vb due to the absence of a clear plane between the gallbladder and the liver.
The surgeon addresses the fistula between the gallbladder and the common bile duct, preparing to manage this connection.
The surgeon dissects the hepatoduodenal ligament to isolate the common bile duct, carefully separating it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon opens the common bile duct anteriorly at the level of the fistula between the gallbladder and the common bile duct, exposing the interior of the duct.
The surgeon isolates the posterior wall from surrounding structures and proceeds to transect it, completing the separation of the gallbladder from adjacent tissues.
The surgeon extracts a large stone from the distal part of the common bile duct, which is visibly opened and isolated from surrounding structures.
The surgeon isolates the distal common bile duct from surrounding structures in preparation for closing the stump.
The surgeon uses a stapler to close the stump of the common bile duct, which has a large caliber.
The surgeon prepares to perform a Roux-en-Y hepaticojejunostomy, indicating the start of the reconstruction phase following the isolation and transection of the bile duct.
The surgeon partially splits the thick omentum and transects the small bowel approximately thirty centimeters distal to the ligament of Treitz.
The surgeon lifts the small bowel in an anti-mesocolic orientation and performs an end-to-side hepatic incision ostomy, creating a visible connection between the bowel and hepatic tissue.
The surgeon begins suturing the posterior layer using a V-lock IV-O absorbable wire, progressing from the medial to the lateral side.
The surgeon creates the posterior layer without opening the small bowel, maintaining the integrity of the bowel during the procedure.
The surgeon uses a barbed suture to maintain continuous traction while suturing the posterior layer in a running fashion, ensuring the layer remains intact and properly aligned.
After completing the posterior layer, the surgeon creates a small enterotomy and begins suturing the anterior layer with a second stitch.
The surgeon sutures the interior layer from medial to lateral, beginning at the same point where the posterior layer suturing commenced, using a continuous technique.
The surgeon uses the previously placed suture for traction while continuing the suturing process.
The completed anastomosis is visible, showing the joined sections of the bowel with sutures securing the anterior and posterior layers.
The surgeon secures the sutures with a hemolog clip, completing the fixation process.
The surgeon restores FENST by creating an enteroenterostomy approximately sixty centimeters distal to the hepatic jejunostomy.
The surgeon creates the posterior layer of the anastomosis using a VLOG-IIO absorbable wire in a running suture technique.
The surgeon opens the small bowel on both sides, revealing the internal lumen and preparing for further surgical manipulation.
The surgeon creates an inner layer of the anastomosis using a VLOG-O absorbable wire, completing the layered structure of the surgical site.
The surgeon completes the inner layer of the anastomosis in a running fashion using absorbable sutures, ensuring a continuous and secure closure.
The surgeon uses a VLOG-O absorbable wire to complete the outer layer on the anterior side of the anastomosis, finalizing the surgical repair.
The surgeon completes the anastomosis, finalizing the connection between the bowel segments.
